content: Stroke is a medical emergency caused by interrupted blood supply to the brain. FAST: Face drooping (asymmetry), Arm weakness (one arm drifts down), Speech difficulty (slurred/strange), Time to call 911. Additional signs: sudden severe headache, vision loss, confusion, balance problems. Two types: ischemic (87%) — clot blocks artery; hemorrhagic (13%) — vessel ruptures. Time is brain: 1.9 million neurons die per minute without treatment. Ischemic stroke window for IV tPA: 4.5 hours; mechanical thrombectomy: up to 24 hours in select patients. Do NOT give aspirin until hemorrhage excluded by CT. Hemorrhagic stroke: control BP, reverse anticoagulation, neurosurgical evaluation. NIHSS scale quantifies neurological deficit. Post-stroke: rehabilitation, secondary prevention (antiplatelets, anticoagulation for AF, BP control, statins).

Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening systemic allergic reaction. Triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, milk), medications (penicillin, NSAIDs, contrast dye), insect stings, latex. Diagnostic criteria: rapid onset involving skin/mucosal tissue PLUS respiratory compromise OR hypotension OR end-organ dysfunction after allergen exposure. Symptoms: urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, stridor, hypotension, syncope, GI symptoms. Treatment: epinephrine IM (0.3-0.5mg of 1:1000) thigh — FIRST LINE, repeat every 5-15 min; call 911; supine position with legs elevated (if no respiratory distress); IV fluid resuscitation; oxygen; H1/H2 antihistamines; corticosteroids (slow onset, adjunctive). Biphasic reaction occurs in 5-20% — observe 4-8 hours. Discharge with auto-injector prescription (EpiPen). Avoid beta-blockers which impair epinephrine response.

Pulmonary embolism is obstruction of pulmonary arteries by thrombus (95% from DVT), fat, air, or amniotic fluid. Risk factors (Virchow's triad): stasis (immobility, AF, heart failure), hypercoagulability (malignancy, OCP, thrombophilia, pregnancy), endothelial injury (surgery, trauma). Classic symptoms: sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia, calf swelling. Wells score and PERC rule for clinical probability. D-dimer sensitive but not specific (high sensitivity excludes PE in low-probability patients). CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is gold standard. Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): systemic thrombolysis (alteplase), catheter-directed thrombolysis, or surgical embolectomy. Sub-massive PE (RV dysfunction without shock): consider systemic thrombolysis if high bleed risk acceptable. Non-massive: anticoagulation (DOACs preferred: rivaroxaban or apixaban, no bridging needed). LMWH for cancer-associated PE. IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated.

DKA is an acute metabolic emergency of type 1 (occasionally type 2) diabetes caused by insulin deficiency leading to hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis. Triad: blood glucose >250 mg/dL, ketones positive (blood or urine), pH <7.3 / bicarbonate <15. Precipitants: infection, insulin omission, new-onset T1D, MI, medications (steroids, SGLT2i). Symptoms: polyuria, polydipsia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, fruity breath, Kussmaul breathing (deep rapid). Management: IV normal saline 1L/hr initially; insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr (after K+ >3.5); potassium replacement (critical — insulin causes hypokalemia); dextrose added when glucose <200; bicarbonate only if pH <6.9; close monitoring glucose, K+, phosphate every 1-2hr. Resolution: glucose <200, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >15, anion gap closed. Transition to SC insulin before stopping infusion.

Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. qSOFA screening: altered mentation, RR ≥22, SBP ≤100. SOFA score quantifies organ dysfunction. Common sources: pneumonia (most common), UTI, abdominal, bacteremia. 1-hour bundle: measure lactate; blood cultures before antibiotics; broad-spectrum antibiotics ASAP; 30mL/kg IV crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4; vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) for persistent hypotension. Source control: drain abscess, remove infected catheter. Reassess fluid responsiveness with dynamic measures. Hydrocortisone for refractory septic shock. Monitor for AKI, ARDS, DIC, hepatic dysfunction. ICU admission for organ support.

Acute respiratory failure is inability to maintain adequate oxygenation or ventilation. Type 1 (hypoxemic): PaO2/FiO2 <300 (ARDS if bilateral infiltrates, non-cardiogenic). Type 2 (hypercapnic): CO2 retention from pump failure (COPD, neuromuscular). ARDS Berlin criteria: onset ≤1 week after clinical insult, bilateral chest opacities not fully explained by fluid/cardiac, PaO2/FiO2 <300 on PEEP ≥5. Causes: pneumonia, aspiration, sepsis, trauma, pancreatitis, transfusions. Treatment: lung-protective ventilation (tidal volume 6mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, permissive hypercapnia), PEEP titration, prone positioning (>12h/day) for PaO2/FiO2 <150, neuromuscular blockade for severe ARDS, conservative fluid strategy after resuscitation, VTE prophylaxis. High-flow nasal cannula or NIV as bridge before intubation if awake and cooperative.

Hypertensive emergency: severe BP elevation (typically >180/120 mmHg) WITH acute target-organ damage — hypertensive encephalopathy, acute MI, unstable angina, acute HF, aortic dissection, acute kidney injury, intracranial hemorrhage, eclampsia. Hypertensive urgency: severe elevation WITHOUT target-organ damage. Emergency management: IV medications (labetalol, nicardipine, sodium nitroprusside, clevidipine, esmolol for aortic dissection). Goal: reduce MAP by 10-20% in first hour, then cautiously to 160/100 over next 2-6 hours — avoid rapid drops causing ischemia. Exception: aortic dissection — target SBP <120 within 20 minutes. Hypertensive urgency: resume/intensify oral medications, close outpatient follow-up — IV therapy rarely needed. Identify and treat underlying cause.

Status epilepticus (SE): continuous seizure activity ≥5 min OR 2+ seizures without return to baseline. Emergency — impending risk at T1 (5 min), irreversible neuronal injury at T2 (30 min). Causes: AED non-compliance, CNS infection, stroke, metabolic (hyponatremia, hypoglycemia), alcohol withdrawal, autoimmune encephalitis. Phase 1 (0-5 min): position patient, protect airway, oxygen, IV access, glucose. First-line (5-20 min): benzodiazepines — IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4mg) OR IM midazolam 10mg OR IV/rectal diazepam. Second-line (20-40 min): IV levetiracetam (60mg/kg) OR fosphenytoin (20 PE/kg) OR valproate (40mg/kg). Refractory SE (>40 min): anesthetic doses — midazolam infusion, propofol, ketamine, pentobarbital coma. EEG monitoring for non-convulsive SE. Treat underlying cause. Post-SE: maintain AED, investigate etiology.

AKI: increase in serum creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6h. KDIGO staging (1-3) guides management. Pre-renal (55%): hypovolemia, sepsis, heart failure → fluid resuscitation, treat underlying cause. Intrinsic (40%): ATN (ischemia/nephrotoxins), glomerulonephritis, interstitial nephritis, rhabdomyolysis. Post-renal (5%): obstruction → catheterize/relieve. Key metrics: urine sodium, FENa <1% (pre-renal), casts on microscopy. Avoid nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast (use iso-osmolar, minimize volume). Treat hyperkalemia (ECG changes: widened QRS, peaked T waves) — calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, sodium bicarbonate, kayexalate, dialysis. Indications for emergent dialysis (AEIOU): Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uremia. Most pre-renal AKI resolves; ATN may take 1-3 weeks.

Aortic dissection: tear in aortic intima allowing blood to enter the media, creating a false lumen. Stanford Type A: involves ascending aorta — surgical emergency. Stanford Type B: descending aorta only — medical management unless complicated. Classic presentation: sudden severe 'tearing' or 'ripping' chest/back pain, maximal at onset, unequal blood pressures in arms (>20 mmHg difference), pulse deficits. Risk factors: hypertension (most common), Marfan/Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine, pregnancy. CT aortography (gold standard), TEE, MRA. Type A: emergency surgery (repair ascending aorta, correct AR, revascularize coronaries). Type B: aggressive BP control (target SBP 100-120 mmHg) with IV beta-blockers (esmolol/labetalol), then oral metoprolol. TEVAR (endovascular repair) for complicated Type B. Avoid thrombolytics — fatal hemorrhage risk.
